sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pulse and respiratory rate are core vital signs that often change early when a patient is becoming unwell. In prehospital care, their trend, quality, and relationship to the whole presentation are usually more important than whether a single reading sits just inside or outside a reference range.

sections.title: Pulse Assessment
sections.content:
- Assess pulse at the site most appropriate to the patient and situation
- Describe rate, rhythm, strength, and whether the pulse is central or peripheral
- In a time-critical patient, note whether perfusion is improving or worsening after treatment
- Irregular rhythm, weak pulse, or loss of peripheral pulses should always be interpreted with the broader haemodynamic picture

sections.title: Respiratory Rate Assessment
sections.content:
- Count respiratory rate accurately whenever possible and avoid estimating it
- Assess depth, pattern, effort, chest movement, and the patient's ability to speak or maintain ventilation
- Respiratory rate is often one of the earliest signs of deterioration
- Reassess after interventions and when the clinical picture changes

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Tachycardia, bradycardia, tachypnoea, or bradypnoea only become meaningful when interpreted in context
- A patient may be critically unwell despite a pulse or respiratory rate that appears near normal
- Pulse quality and work of breathing often add more value than the number alone
- Always document the time and any major change from previous observations
